title: Moonshot Podcast Deep Dive: Sebastian Thrun on Waymo's ...
text:
When the the self-driving car project,
which is at the time was called
Chauffeur, now was the company Whimo,
was kicked off, the world didn't believe
it was even possible for there to be
self-driving cars on the road. And so,
in many senses, the world was not ready
for it. I didn't believe it was
possible. I had seen these desert
vehicles running into cactuses and other
stuff. I had every reason to believe
this is fundamentally impossible. Sorry,
just to be honest here. Yeah. And I was
the expert. Like I was the expert not to
be trusted.
Sebastian, I'm so excited we're getting
to do this together. So am I. Thank you,
my friend. My brother. Yes. Oh my god.
We have known each other for so long. It
is crazy. Almost 30 years we've known
each other. It's incredible. But I
thought maybe we would start um about
two two and a half years before uh you
got X up and running and asked me to
come and like co- set it up with you. Um
which is what was at the time called Vue
Tool.
You and some graduate students were
doing something which you at the time
were calling Vue at Stanford and you
asked me if I would come spin it out and
be the CEO. We got really excited and it
didn't quite work. Can you sort of
explain how that led into you getting
into Google? My god, a man with a
memory. Holy moly. Um, we were at uh
Stanford University. I was uh teaching
as a professor in computer science and I
always gave my grad students interesting
project assignments and one was can you
just photograph all of San Francisco and
put it on a on a a smartphone. Um that
was effectively what later became street
view. We didn't know that yet and uh the
student was his name did an outstanding
job. Uh so we decided maybe it's a
spinout. Um, when I thought about this,
I had a few students at Stanford I
wanted to bring in and my very first
thought went to you, Astra, because
you're the most amazing entrepreneur I
knew at the time and even today. So, I
called you up and say, "Are you
interested in coming to Silicon Valley
and be a CEO of a new startup company?"
And that was amazing. You came
immediately. That was great.
But then before we could incorporate it,
Google basically purchased the rights
even though it hadn't been incorporated
yet. So, yeah, we were very lucky. So it
turns out 2007 the context was it was
just a year away from the biggest crash
in the financial world would have been
impossible to build a company without a
business model and I was so lucky that I
knew Larry Page the one of the Google
co-founders and while we are raising
money on central road from various very
seasoned venture firms we also talked to
Larry Page and he said hey come to
Google and so you and I said oh shoot we
almost got to work together but we
didn't because it hadn't even been
incorporated so you and your students
students went into Google and I went off
and I did something else for about two
years. And during that two years where
you were taking a break from being a
professor from Stanford, you helped not
only set up Street View, but you helped
in some other pretty substantial ways at
Google and really impressed Larry and
Sergey. So, they really didn't want you
to leave.
You had also won the DARPA Grand
Challenge while at Stanford, sort of the
early proof that self-driving cars maybe
were ready or getting ready to be
makeable. I remember um my first job was
to build up street view and there was an
existing team very competent and
together we we really scaled up this
thing, built new cameras, drove many
many streets and so on and after about a
year or so we decided to build Google's
map data, navigational data. So, we took
the street view asset and massaged it
into a navigable street map, allowing
Android smartphones to give turnbyturn
directions, which back in the day was
actually a big deal. This is 2007, 2008.
The iPhone had just shown up and no one
yet had understood that smartphones can
give directions. My memory is that Larry
Page came to me and said, "Sastian, I
thought about this heart. We should
build a self-driving car that could
navigate everywhere in California." And
I was the person who was the the
negative guy. He was like, "Oh my god,
that's really hard." And for people who
don't remember 2009, let me just put
this into context. 2005, you mentioned
it, Astro Dapa Grand Challenge, a robot
race organized by the US government
where robots were driven in the desert
in empty desert and most robots made it
maybe 10 miles, 15, 20 miles in distance
before they went up in flames. So for
me, thinking about building a car that
could drive down Lombard Street in San
Francisco
sounded like homicide. And then Larry
would insisted and said, "Look,
Sebastian, I thought about it hard.
Maybe in the beginning it's not quite
safe yet. You can have a safety driver,
but as you progress, um, you can make it
safer and safer." Um, and I was correct,
but I still felt, "Oh my god, that's so
insanely hard. We can't do it." And
eventually Larry convinced me and said,
"Look, even it can't be done. Why didn't
I want to try it? And then it was more
the technical challenge that kept me
there than any kind of system or or
entity to build or reward whatever was
really the idea that we could try to
build a truly self-driving car which at
the time was completely impossible.
Tell me a little bit about like so the
the technical challenge I really get why
that excited you but I know that you
were also passionate about kind of the
problem itself like and the benefits to
the world. Tell me a little bit about
that side of of your interest and your
excitement about it. Yeah, like
everybody else, I'm a user of cars. Uh
it's probably the biggest invention in
the 20th century because it really
transformed cities and and labor and
independence and women's right and all
that stuff. But it also kills a million
a million two people every year. Okay.
And one of those people who was was my
friend Harold when I was 18 who died in
a traffic accident. And it was a pivotal
moment in my life where I realized, oh
my god, we just kill people. Like we
worry about a plane crash last week or a
few weeks ago in in DC where what 60 70
people died. This happens every morning
for breakfast on Americans highways if
we just don't worry about it as much. So
I felt like if you could make cars
safer, it would have a great benefit to
society.
And how how did you then step up from uh
self-driving cars to kind of the
opportunity to set up a 21st century
Bell Labs at at Google like tell me
about how that conversation went between
you and well the credit should all go to
you Astro not to myself but um the
founders of Google Larry and Sergey who
I admire indefinitely really believed
that at the edge of technology society's
underinvesting and that great new
businesses could be built. Self-driving
cars being just one of them. And in
their opinion, a self-driving car
company could be as big as Google was at
the time. And and that's actually quite
true today. Um so they asked me,
Sebastian, can we build more crazy
businesses at the age of technology
where we have an insight that something
might actually work? It's not working
yet. And then they asked me, Sebastian,
would you be interested in being
involved right as running it? and they
had been looking for many years for the
right person. I'm not sure I was the
right person, but I raised my arm and
said, "Give it a try." And uh the rest
is history. And then you came in
December of 2009 and asked if I would
set it up with you. That's true. You're
a great people person. You're infinitely
smart. You're a technology optimist.
You're much deeper than anybody else I
know. I was so delighted that you came
and became my partner in this. And we I
think we have equal partners from the
get-go. You know, the way I usually
describe it is since I now call myself
the captain of moonshots is that for the
first two plus years, I was the first
mate. I mean, I I appreciate you saying
we were partners. I reported to you and
I remember that you only gave me one
really concrete piece of advice, which
you gave me on my very first day working
at X. We hadn't even named it yet, which
was ignore your manager, which was you.
And it worked out well. It did work out
well. Hope it was a good piece of
advice. Tell me a little bit about what
you hoped that this place could be or
what you hoped it wouldn't turn into. So
I I studied um labs in preparation of my
conversation with the founders and the
one that appealed the most to me uh was
Loit Martin and Skunkorks where small
groups of people were able to go out
into this like smelly building that's
why it's called skunkorks and work on an
idea without being distracted by
management by finance by reporting all
that kind of stuff. and they invented
amazing stuff in this in this world is
always focused on business on building a
new business a new capability it was
always people who got together for the
purpose of in innovation there were no
no lifetime scientists I've seen a lot
of lifetime researchers fighting for
whatever was hot during their PhD thesis
at times when the world moved on I
didn't want permanent staff I didn't
want publicity I wanted clear focus on
let's solve a really hard problem that
we into a new business and that to me um
having the chance to build this up was
amazing. I think we really succeeded. We
built amazing businesses and amazing
technologies.
Maybe tell me a little bit about some of
your either fondest memories, funniest
memories, um you know, some of the
things that you think were like just
really important early kind of tone
setters. Like for example, I remember
that the Larry 1000, which was a set of
challenges, 1000mile
routes that if the Whimo team could
accomplish, they would get a lot more
funding, they would get a reward, and
there was something about that that just
sort of set a really nice tone. Do you
have things like that either on the
Whimo team for Glass, for other things,
Google Brain? Yes. So what we the way I
set it up is I always wanted to have a
clarity on what is where we're going and
that's actually surprisingly challenging
in the corporate America. I'd learned
this uh at this thing called Dapper
Grand Challenge where we had a robot
race where on a specific day in 2005 we
had to drive a robot to drive 140 miles
and there was non-negotiable. So if you
came a day late, tough luck. There was
no excuse to be made. And that level of
clarity helped us getting ready for this
competition a month early and then
eventually win it. Um I wanted the same
winning at Google X. I wanted clarity
about where we're going. And the best
way to codify is was to set ourselves
milestones and those milestones always
were negotiated with a lot of work, a
lot of thought. In the self-driving car
in Whimo, it was drive a thousand miles
that Larry and Sergey had personally
identified to be the hardest roads in
California. They said for hours in front
of Google, this is really hard. Lomod
street. Okay. Okay. We got to drive L
street and and they negotiate with me.
It has to be entirely hands-off, 100%
autonomous. Um the the benefit of this
was mostly clarity for the team. So the
team could go to work and say this is
what we're going to build. There was no
argument anymore. to build this or that.
On Whimo, for example, there was an
extended period during which there was
both the uh Prius, which was the initial
platform for learning how to drive that
the team had hacked so that it could
drive by wire, but there was also the
golf cart sort of side exploration.
How do you think about that tradeoff
between having ultra focus for where you
really should go but also you don't
totally know where you should go or even
how to accomplish the milestone. So
there was a lot of kind of creativity in
those early days, wasn't there? The way
I set it up when I ran it is I want
always wanted the clarity in a written
down milestone which effectively became
a contractual document um where we
really say this is the technical thing
we have to accomplish for this thing to
be successful. uh when you go into the
business world and ask about customer um
kind of engagement, it's harder to cover
the specific milestone because it's less
predictable. But while you're building
technology, a milestone is incredibly
useful. There's been cases where we
didn't live up to it and crazy people
came with crazy ideas. I don't know. But
as long as the milestone was clear,
execution was really great in Google X.
H ultimately you left to become the CEO
of Udacity,
but there was a period where you know
you at least dabbled with the idea of
doing an education moonshot within
Google X maybe a little bit. What are
your thoughts on like do you think
Udacity thrived better for being
outside? Um like what excited you about
an education moonshot? Well, uh, while I
was, um, at X, I put a course online at
Stanford. The course was an artificial
intelligence level course. At the time,
AI was not a big deal. Today, it is.
And, uh, within like a days, we signed
up 160,000 learners from around the
world, which is a a massive number. So,
it became clear to me that there is
appetite, a massive insatible appetite
for education in the world. and my
students started the company called
Udacity that I later much later joined.
Um, was it better or worse? Udacity sold
to Accentia very nicely. So the outcome
was great. It has a massive footing in
the Middle East. There's now millions of
people's lives changed because of it. So
I'm very proud of it. Imagine for a
second that I'm going to start my first
day at X and you are the original like
moonshot leader. Uh, and even after
you've left, you've done some things
that, you know, arguably are moonshot
worthy. What What's your advice for
young prospective moonshot takers? Like
what would you tell me either if I was
going to X or even if I was somewhere
else, but I wanted to like really shoot
over the horizon, particularly
audacious, like what are some of the
things that you've learned about how to
do that best? Well, anybody on this
podcast listening in um it's really
important to um realize how society is
progressing today through technology. Um
society is or people are about 300,000
years old and almost anything we care
about today from electric light switches
to flushing toilets is really not older
than 150 years. Cars, cell phones and so
on, general anesthesia like stuff that
we take for granted today. Um that to me
means that probably less than 1% of
interesting things have been invented
yet and 99% of someone else to invent.
Okay? And if you don't believe it, just
look at the last three or four years
with AI and social networks and all that
stuff. The world is massively changing.
So pick something that you believe in
that can be done. Technologically pretty
much everything can be done. Uh almost
nothing cannot be done. And that's
actually a surprising thing because most
of us in our mindset believe we we love
the past invention. We love our
smartphones and all that stuff, but we
kind of level off and can't imagine that
new things are being invented. So the
first thing is pick a problem you care
about like your grandmother cares about
like in my case the death of my friend
Harold and then ask yourself can you
envision a technology that can solve
this problem and then work on it. Uh in
all likelihood it can be solved. In all
likelihood, the problem is not that we
don't try. We don't have technology. The
likelihood is we don't try hard enough
to solve these problems.
Um, I love it the way you just said
that. And as you just pointed out,
almost anything that that could be made
can be made, but not everything that can
be made should be made. Any thought as
we sort of rush into the future about
the obligation of technologists to sort
of help bend the world towards the
positive? Like at at what point does the
technologist responsibility end? And and
up to what point do the technologist
have some sort of ethical or moral
responsibility to make sure that the
things they're creating are actually
going to be good for humanity? It's very
very simple. First, everything that can
be made will be made. Whether you say it
should or should not be made is
immaterial because it will be made. Our
job is not to stop making things. Our
job is to
prevent the misuse of things that we
make. Overall, technology has been
absolutely amazing. It has been a
democratizing force in the world in
every aspect in terms of longevity,
health, mobility, education, whatever
food. There's no more problems with
obesity and malnutrition. All of a
sudden in first time in history of
humanity extreme poverty is
disappearing. Technology is a great
force for the better. But every single
person including technologies have a
responsibility. It's a not just a
technology question. It's a societal
question. We have to involve like
politicians and and and lots of people
from society because it transforms all
of us and we jointly have to be
transformed. But having said this, I'm
an optimist. like just the technologies
in my lifetime, street view,
self-driving cars or stuff I didn't do
myself like I smartphones have been so
transformational that I'm super
optimistic.
Uh
I agree. Uh let's get a little bit more
specific. Where do you think artificial
intelligence and machine learning are
going? The technology is changing the
world very fast, but no part of it is
changing the world nearly as quickly as
the ramp up in, you know, what most
listeners would understand as foundation
models or large language models, though
obviously artificial intelligence is a
lot more than just that particular way
of trying to get computers to learn from
experience. Where do you think all
that's going? First of all, every single
person I know uses almost on a daily
basis. use it for really productive deep
things like drafting letters or doing
research or dinner reservation
recommendations or travel
recommendations. Let's move faster.
There's a lot of sentiment that comes in
um around this idea of fear of the new
and I I respect this. But in reality,
we're building technology empowers
people be more productive and more more
we're building technology that gives a
skill set of a small number of people to
a large number of people. So every
single person now has medical skills
they didn't have before, has lawyer
skills they didn't have before, finance
skills they didn't have before,
information skills. So we are actually
doing something amazing in the world
with these new large language models,
but by and large the benefits have been
very positive. What do you think? So
obviously many jobs will go away, lots
more jobs will come into existence. How
do you think society can kind of like
bend towards the positive as all this
change unrolls? If you go back in
history and use history as a guidepost,
some 300 years ago, we had no loons. We
had no steam engine. And most of us
people worked in farming because one
farmer could make food for four people.
Today, a farmer can make food for 400
people. Now, did the jobs go away? I
don't think anybody sees it this way.
Yes, jobs did go away. Farming jobs
massively disappeared. But for most of
us, we have new jobs. Massage
therapists, pilots, TV anchor, software
engineers. There's so many more jobs.
Okay. When we build new technology that
amplifies people, we create more jobs
and more diverse jobs and give people
more an ability to self-determine and
give people more independence and make
the world a much much better place. The
same will happen now. So there are
inefficiencies in my own work. Half the
work I do I would love to get rid of.
And if AI could just do it for me, I'd
be so happy. And if that means I can
spend twice as much time with my kids,
all the better. Okay? And maybe I work
harder again because do something new.
Let's figure this out. But society will
be better off as a result.
Going back to X for a minute, what were
some of the things in the early days of
X where you thought
that's not going to work and it did turn
out to be a good idea or vice versa
where you know we thought it was going
to be a good idea and then it turned out
afterwards not to be a good idea. Well,
so one thing happened uh before you
actually joined uh Yokim Matsuko and I
went to Larry and Ser and we had this
cool new idea that you could listen to
every conversation everywhere by putting
a small tiny magnet in your ear. And the
way we found this out was a cheating
website. You could actually buy a small
magnet like tenth of an inch, put in
your ear and put an inductive loop
around your neck, put this in your cell
phone, and then someone could speak to
you and the inductive loop would
activate the magnet to become basically
a little tiny speaker and you could
listen to someone while you take an
exam. And as a result, you could cheat.
And we felt it be such a great consumer
product. Everybody puts a magnet in the
ear in the morning and now they can
connect to their cell phone, never have
to pick the phone out of the pocket and
still have a conversation uh using their
cell phone. Um end up not to be a good
idea. So after
put the magnet in the ear and tried it
out, it was really hard to get it out
again. though that's an interesting
example because then after um I started
one of the things which we tried for
almost a year which I'm sure you
remember was called siren which was like
machine mediated hearing that would
block up your ear entirely but there
were uh speakers inside your ear canal
and outside so that it could decide what
went into your ear and what didn't you
remember that I do remember that and
then we closed it down we decided that
that wasn't right but many years later
that started up again at X was
internally was called Wolverine. It then
spun out. It's now called IO on the
outside. And the wireless version of
that is coming out in just a few months.
And so I think that that thing that you
envisioned is actually now ready for
prime time. It's pretty cool. There's an
amazing um shift in society when you
zoom out and look for mainframe
computers all the way to to smartphones
where the compute and interfacing gets
closer and closer to a natural senses
and the ultimate will be right now for
our set of glasses and something that
sits in your ear around your ear so that
the interface to the virtual world
becomes as fluent as the physical world
and that was the intent with Google
Glass. We really wanted to build the
very first version of it, but there's
many more iterations to invent it. And
when that happens, we're going to be in
a wonderful situation where all the
online wisdom is and so on guidance can
guide physical real interactions with
people and places and so on. That might
sound scary, but it's actually going to
be quite amazing in my books. I agree.
Uh in the early days um of X, you know,
I sometimes describe it now in
retrospect. I I think of it as like it's
really easy to take moonshots if you
don't care about efficiency. You just
throw a lot of money at high energy
people and you'll get some great stuff.
It's just not very efficient. It's easy
to be super efficient. You just are get
arbitrarily uptight and you have lots of
efficiency, but there'll be no magic.
The trick is finding somewhere in
between. And I at least in retrospect
see X as having started fairly far
towards the Wild West end of the
spectrum. And the process we've been
going through over the last 15 years is
very slowly trying to turn up the rigor
without losing the magic. And when I
think about those early days, I think
there was a lot of of joy, of fun, of
creativity in that almost wild westlike
uh entrepreneurial go fast, see how how
fast we can get to like good things in
the world. Um, I'll give you an example,
but I'm looking for some from you. I
remember we wrote up on the wall at some
point that the event horizon would
between would be when we were moving
your desk location faster than you could
sit in it and so that there was at least
one desk location for you Sebastian that
you never actually got a chance to sit
in before it changed again
because I remember in those early days
we were constantly trying to jam more
people in. Do you remember some things
like that to sort of capture the feeling
of those really early days? I mean
obviously in the early days uh we had no
finance people on board. Yeah. We had a
moratorium on powerpoints, no reporting.
We were ultra secret. Google didn't know
we existed except for a small number of
people. Regular googers could not use
their badge to open the door to us. So
we are really operating in complete
secrecy. Um for the teams that I hired
and and team leaders I would tell them
look you might be inclined to hire many
many people but managing many people is
a lot of work don't do that okay hire
yourself few people you really trust you
know um
obviously Larry and Sergey were super
involved and and super important for X
in the early days. Um, the other two
people who I remember at least somewhat
involved were Bill Corin and um, Eric
Schmidt. Were there other people besides
the two of them that you felt like had
some impact or was really just the four
of them? Um, yeah. So, Bill Corin was my
manager and he was known to be Google's
best manager because he had only one
thing is when I cried, he listened.
I remember at some point I needed a
promotion and I showed it up to the
committee that that that you didn't need
a promotion. You wanted someone else. I
wanted someone else to be promoted.
Correct. Yes. Thank you. I saw and Bill
saw me and said, "Go away. You have more
important things to do. I'll take care
of that for you." There was my manager,
Bill. He was amazing. And uh he taught
me management. Like management is not
about accountability and all that stuff.
Management is about removing roadblocks
and really empowering your people. And I
took this with me for the rest of my
life. Yeah. And I remember that in the
early days um when I asked about
budgeting, you essentially were like,
"Pretend you have no budget. Go do
something interesting." Yeah. Which was
sort of your way of saying, "Don't worry
about rules. That's the wrong attitude
right now." Yeah. So, I think budget is
an interesting thing because people are
misinced. Like when you get two more
headcounts, you're going to fill them as
fast as possible. They could go away. Uh
I think it was the wrong way to think
about it. You should not think of
filling headcounts. You should do the
opposite. You should when you're
desperate to need somebody, you know
you're desperate. Hire that person.
Okay? And that's all you should do. I
just didn't like this idea of of
PowerPoint updates. The problem with
PowerPoints in general is that every
manager in the reporting chain has a
different opinion. And they all want to
take credit, right? So that what does a
manager do? 90% is taking credit. So
think about normal managers, not you and
me, of course.
Think about like if you have like four
or five levels of management that I used
to have like every single person like
Bill had a manager called Alen Ellen had
a manager and so on. Um every person
will listen to this and be smart about
it and say here's a new idea right
here's a way to change it and the poor
engineers at the bottom they capture all
these ideas and all of a sudden this
very pristine small milestone has become
this bloated massive thing and they
can't execute that the chain of
management makes things more complex. So
I just said it stops here. I don't want
an update. Call me in when you need
help. Otherwise, I'm going to stay out
of your hair here and do your job. Reach
your milestone.
Uh, anything else before we go out and
hail a Whimo cab that you'd love the
listeners to understand about the the
early days of X, about some of the
misadventures,
I don't know, a special person or two
that you remember fondly. Oh my god. Uh,
I can't tell the audience how amazing
you are, Astro. And what a great partner
you were. Astro was my human interface
where I like empathy. He had actually
had empathy to bring and and all this on
top of the same in intellectual mind and
and strength that the best people have
in the world. Um so the ex would not
have exist without you. And of course we
had a third partner who left very
quickly, Yoki. She was also great. Um
and we had a fantastic team like we
probably got 20 or 30 professors out of
the professor job in the industry. We
shouldn't be proud about this because
academia is important. But many people
came to us and said this is a better
environment than my home university. I
don't have to do committees and
admissions and teaching all the stuff I
don't like. I can just focus on what I
care about. And the velocity was much
much higher. The number of patents
written was much higher. That was really
amazing. Um I think a few other things
we did well. I think um we had a no
publication rule. Stop publishing. Uh I
didn't publish a single paper Violet X.
I don't know about yourself. I didn't
either. And think about this. I'm an
academic. I published hundreds of papers
before. Um, but the the problem with
papers was the moment you start
publishing, you're kind of in it for
yourself and not for the entity anymore.
And and frankly speaking, every employee
is conflicted. They want to work for the
company that pays them, but they also
are in there for themselves. And that's
fair, right? We ask for a raise and so
on. We want to have a vacation, what
have you, right? But with papers, if you
